coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized with Bovine Serum Albumin for sensing of hypochlorite ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Ekta
Vashisht, Devika
Thakur, Vaishali
Vashisht, Aseem
Mehta, S.K.
Singh, Kulvinder
coredata.dc:description: We present a simple and facile strategy for the fabrication of BSA (Bovine Serum Albumin) functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (BQDs) via the hydrothermal method. BQDs were synthesized using BSA and citric acid. Optical and morphological investigations were carried out to examine the structure BQDs. The photoluminescence of synthesized BQDs was observed to decay upon the addition of hypochlorite ions. There is absence of any interference due to competitive ions. Limit of detection (LOD) comes out to be 35.39 μM for hypochlorite ions in aqueous system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Optical sensing
Hydrothermal method
Hypochlorite
Graphene Quantum Dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent carbonaceous quantum dots
Anion recognition studies for hypochlorite ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis Absorption spectrum of BQDs (b) PL spectra of the effect of different excitations on the emission of BQDs (c) Effect of Excitation on the intensity of BQDs (d) Effect of pH on the emission of BQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of BQDs (b) XRD pattern of BQDs (c) TEM images of BQDs (Low magnification), inset of (c) Size distribution histogram (d) TEM images of BQDs (High magnification).

(a) Selectivity studies of BQDs (b) Effect of several metal ions on the emission intensity of BQDs (c) Effect of hypochlorite ions sensing on the PL emission of BQDs (d) Detail effect of hypochlorite ions on PL emission of BQDs.
Stern Volmer on (a) for hypochlorite (b) Linearity in stern Volmer for hypochlorite (c) Effect of pH on hypochlorite ion sensing (d) Time studies for hypochlorite ion.
(a) Interference Studies (b) Graphical representation of the mechanism for sensing of hypochlorite ions.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized with Bovine Serum Albumin for sensing of hypochlorite ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Basic and Applied Sciences, Maharaja Agrasen University, Baddi, 174103, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh, 160010, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh 160010, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh, 160010, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a simple and facile strategy for the fabrication of BSA (Bovine Serum Albumin) functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (BQDs) via the hydrothermal method. BQDs were synthesized using BSA and citric acid. Optical and morphological investigations were carried out to examine the structure BQDs. The photoluminescence of synthesized BQDs was observed to decay upon the addition of hypochlorite ions. There is absence of any interference due to competitive ions. Limit of detection (LOD) comes out to be 35.39 μM for hypochlorite ions in aqueous system.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the years, Quantum Dots (QDs) have drawn immense interest and have served as promising alternatives to the conventional phosphor material/nanomaterials due to their exceptional opto-electronic properties [1]. However, most of the literature reports focus on the QDs composed of metallic elements such as ZnO, CdS, CdTe, CdSe, ZnSe, ZnS [1,2]. Due to the high fabrication cost and toxic nature of metal-based QDs, they find limited application in biomedical as well as environmental science. In this context, carbonaceous QDs have some distinctive features and a variety of applications because of their low toxicity, biocompatibility, easy functionalization and great chemical stability [3,4]. In addition to this, QDs have required fluorescent properties having high quantum yield that leads to excessive utilization in the fields of photocatalysis, bioimaging, photovoltaic cells, light-emitting diodes and optical sensors [3–5]. Out of the various applications, optical sensing is the most explored application [6–9]. Carbonaceous QDs particularly carbon quantum dots i.e. CDs have been widely explored as an optical sensor due to their unmatched properties such as ease of synthesis, functionalization, high extinction coefficient [10,11] etc. Tan et al. synthesized carbon dots for monitoring of Pb(II) and Cu(II) by the carbonization of industrial waste by pyrolysis method [12]. Deng et al. produced fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection based on Rhodamine 6G moieties. These 6G moieties coupled with covalent bonds onto the surface of CDs [13]. Liu et al. developed highly selective nitrogen-based CDs for mercury(II) ion detection with an excellent quantum yield of 40.5% [14]. To further improve the selectivity of the carbonaceous quantum dots various efforts have been such as functionalization of QDs, molecular imprinting etc. [15–17]. Masteri-Farahani et al. have developed the S and N doped graphene quantum dots functionalized with boric acid and developed a nano sensors for glucose monitoring with the detection limit of 5.5 μM [15]. Similarly, surface modification of graphene quantum dots with antimorphine leads to the development of optical sensors for the detection of morphine [16]. A step further, methamphetamine have been successfully detected by the cavity engraved over the graphene quantum dots with the aid of imprinting polymer with excellent selectivity and sensitivity [17].

Wei and group fabricated carbon dots and developed real-time detection of hypochlorite in body fluids [18]. Xue et al. developed low-cost nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of free chlorine in aqueous system [19]. Reactive oxygen species are the regulators for various chemical and biological processes. Hypochlorite is also an essential reactive oxygen species that is extensively utilized as domestic bleach, cleaning of drinking water, cyanide treatment and for reducing the temperature during water treatment [20]. The concentration of hypochlorite generally used is in the range 10000–0.1 mM that is hazardous to animal and human health [21]. Hypochlorite plays a fundamental role in the tissues of living beings but at the same time, its high level causes damage to the immune system of living organism [22]. Thus, it is important to effectively monitor hypochlorite ions both qualitatively as well as quantitatively. Recently, fluorescent sensors are used for sensing a trace amount of material in the organic and natural samples [23,24]. Lin et al. have designed fluorescent sensing probes for hypochlorite sensing built on deoximation reaction [25]. Therefore, it is essential to monitor the higher concentration of hypochlorite. Numerous reports on the detection of hypochlorite ion through fluorescence sensor are published [21,26,27] but still, there are some gaps such as low selectivity, poor sensitivity and are time-consuming processes. Therefore, it is important to design an appropriate and less expensive process for qualitative and quantitative analysis of hypochlorite.

Herein, we report the hydrothermal route for BSA functionalized graphene quantum dots (BQDs) synthesis where citric acid and BSA have been employed as precursor. BSA due to its biocompatibility, various functional groups, ease of binding helps to develop an efficient optical sensor by functionalization with various carbonaceous quantum dots [28]. As fabricated BQDs have highly luminescent, have tremendous dispersibility with little polydispersity. The Photoluminescence (PL) intensity of as synthesized BQDs showed quenching in the presence of Hypochlorite ion which shows its efficacy as an optical sensor for hypochlorite ions detection. The present study describes a novel method using biocompatible BSA as starting precursor for synthesizing C-dots. Simple and efficient hydrothermal approach has been employed for fabrication of QDs. BSA was carefully chosen as it has less toxic and more biocompatible nature which will evade the probability of contamination.

BSA and citric acid were purchased from the commercial supplier Sigma Aldrich (now Merck) and were used as received without further purification. Metal salts were procured from Alfa Aesar and their aqueous solutions were prepared in distilled water. To eliminate any interference from the glassware, it was washed properly using aqua regia (HCl: HNO3:1:3). HCl, NaOH and HNO3 were ordered from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd.

The stretching vibrations for various bonds were measured using Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption in the range of 200–800 nm was measured using a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette of 10 mm path length on a Thermo Scientific UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence emission response was measured using Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer. Digital P200 pH meter from Cole-Parmer was used to measure the pH of the solution to carry out pH studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent carbonaceous quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For BQDs, 10 mL homogeneous aqueous solution containing 0.10 g of BSA and 0.2 g of Citric acid was prepared in a glass beaker. The mixture was transferred to Teflon line hydrothermal chamber after stirring it for 5 min. The hydrothermal was tightly sealed and placed in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 3 h. The reaction yellow-colored reaction mixture was then placed to achieve room temperature and kept in dark for further use. Synthesis of unmodified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is given supplementary data section 1.

After synthesis, to explore the BQDs, optical, morphological and crystalline investigations were carried out. As evident from Fig. 1 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the BQDs shows two wide absorption bands which are characteristic of carbonaceous quantum dots.

The first band at 336 nm refers to n→π* transition which could be allocated to the CO and C–O groups present in parent molecules. There is other shoulder centered at 259 nm referred to π→π* transition mainly attributable to the sp2 type of carbon present in the BQDs [29–31]. Similarly, when unmodified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was exposed under UV–vis. spectrum two were observed i.e. a shoulder at 280 nm due to π→π* and a peak at 357 nm due to n→π* (Fig. S1). It was observed that absorption peak of BQDs are blue shifted as compare to GQDs reveals the decrease in size of the quantum dots with the introduction of BSA in the system [32]. In addition, the intensity of absorption of GQDs is less then BQDs indicates that the concentration of quantum dots formation is less in case of GQDs. The prepared GQDs and BQDs also displayed excitation dependent fluorescence behavior. As the excitation intensity was increased, the PL intensity also started increasing up to a particular point when the intensity became maximum in case of BQDs but in case of GQDs the PL intensity increase and then vanishes. However, with further increase in wavelength, the PL intensity decreased in case of BQDs (Fig. 1(b–c) and Fig. S2). The as-prepared GQDs and BQDs were exposed to a range of excitation wavelength starting from 280 nm to 400 nm and recorded the responses of emission. Fig. S2 displays the PL emission of GQDs and Fig. 1(c) shows the PL intensity of BQDs at different wavelengths. The maximum intensity of absorption was observed at 340 nm for both GQDs and BQDs. Therefore, the emission spectrum of GQDs and BQDs was found to be dependent on excitation which lead to the fact that carbonaceous quantum dots exhibited photoluminescence due to surface defective sites owing to surface passivation on the surface of quantum dots [33]. In addition, when both BQDs and GQDs were exposed under UV chamber, it was observed that the GQDs shows very low intensity of blue emission as compare to BQDs revealing that the presence of BSA enhances the PL emission quantum dots (Fig. S3). The PL spectrum of BQDs was also examined under different pH ranging from 1.0 to 12.0 and an irregular behavior was observed. The PL intensity first started increasing with a rise in pH range from 1.0 to 2.0 and then decreased and after that almost similar intensities were observed (Fig. 1(d)). This occurrence was possibly attributed to different protonation ratios of BQDs at different pH. Under high acidity (pH < 2) the pronation was over BQDs which resulted in the decrease of PL intensity and at pH > 2 the ratio of protonation started to decrease to some extent. In an extremely basic medium (pH = 12) the intensity also decreased which lead to the deprotonation of the functional groups present. From the pH, 3.0 to 11.0 maximum intensities were observed which lead to the fact that these BQDs have determined applicability for a wide range of pHs which is suitable for application in various fields particularly in medical and other sensing techniques [34].

For a detailed examination of surface of GQDs and BQDs for presence of functional groups, FT-IR studies were executed (Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. S4). Existence of peaks at wavenumbers (cm−1) 3295 (O–H/N–H), 2939 (C–H stretching), 1701 (CO), 1632 (CH), 1389 (C–NH), 1357 (CN), 1172 (C–N stretching) and at 899 cm−1 indicated the presence of parent entities and different functional groups similarly, FITR Spectrum of GQDs displays peaks at wavenumber (cm−1) 788 (CH2), 1230 (C–OH), 1400 (CH2), 1728 (CO), and a broad peak at 3420 cm−1 reveal the presence of hydrogen bonding in O–H group [10,33,35,36].

TEM studies depicted that the BQDs be well dispersed in water and well separated from each other (Fig. 2 (c-d)). These studies also revealed the BQDs to be below 10 nm in size with very low polydispersity [37]. The size histogram (inset of Fig. 2(c)) indicated that BQDs have an average size of about 5.4 nm in and diameter ranging from 2 to 11 nm. XRD studies were acquired to investigate the crystallinity of BQDs. Fig. 2(b) illustrates the XRD pattern of BQDs with 2θ = 20°, which indicated the amorphous nature and as per the previous studies conducted on carbonaceous material. The slight broad peak between 10° and 20° is due to the biochemical BSA present on the surface of BQDs [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anion recognition studies for hypochlorite ion
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the specific nature of BQDs was examined in the absence and presence of various ions (Sodium Carbonate, Sodium Citrate, Sodium Sulfite, Sodium tetra hydridoborate, Sodium Chloride, Sodium Fluoride, Sodium Bicarbonate, Sodium thiocyanate, Sodium phosphate, Sodium Acetate, Sodium Hypochlorite, Sodium Nitrite, Sodium Oxalate, Sodium thiosulfate, Sodium Sulfide, Sodium Sulfate). No significant changes were observed with any of the species, selected but, as Fig. 3 (a) depicts, the high response was observed only in the case of hypochlorite ion. It was analyzed that the intensity of PL quenched upon the addition of hypochlorite ion and relatively very low responses were observed for other ionic species. This showed the selectivity of the detection system specifically for sodium hypochlorite ion.

Fig. 3(b) shows the selectivity, rapid and stronger binding affinity of BQDs towards hypochlorite ions. Fig. 3(c) indicates the effects of hypochlorite ion concentration on PL spectra of BQDs. BQDs were exposed to different concentrations of hypochlorite. The intensity of BQDs decreased as the concentration of hypochlorite was increased [40]. Fig. 3(d) indicates the decay of PL intensity with an increase in concentration. The plot of PL intensity versus concentration was established to fit in the Gaussian model (y = y0 + (A/(w*sqrt (PI/2)))*exp(-2*((x-xc)/w)^2)) with R2 = 0.9947 which displays a good fitting.

To explore the sensing mechanism of hypochlorite ion, stern volume equation as shown in Eq. (1)
(1)
F 0
/ F =
K
s v
[ Q ]
+ 1
Where
F o
is the fluorescence intensity of blank (without the addition of analyte),
F
is the fluorescence intensity afterwards the addition of analyte,
K
s v
is the Stern Volmer constant and
Q
is the concentration of quencher. Fig. 4(a) shows the exponential fit with the value of R2 = 0.934.

There are various reasons for the decrease in PL intensity such as transfer of energy or photoinduced electron which induce the delay of fluorescence intensity. The quenching data fits into the linear model and show an excellent fitting as R2 = 0.99112 (Fig. 4(b)). The dynamic and static quenching are responsible for the PL quenching response. The complex formed at the ground state (with analyte) was responsible for static quenching and when excited luminescent molecule collides with quencher dynamic quenching occurred. At higher concentration, there is competition between dynamic and static quenching due to which upward creature occurs [33]. The limit of detection calculated for hypochlorite is 35.39 μM and also the Ksv value for hypochlorite ion is 5.85 × 106 M−1. To analyze the effect of pH on the hypochlorite ion sensing, pH studies were conducted. (Fig. 4(c)) shows the plot of pH versus relative intensity (F0/F) where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence and F are the fluorescence intensity of BQDs in the presence of hypochlorite ion. The pHs of different solutions were adjusted by using HCl and NaOH. The concentration of analyte was fixed at different pH. In the case of hypochlorite, the relative intensity is maximum in the acidic pH range as nitrogen gets protonated which gave rise to dynamic quenching [41]. Fig. 4(d) displays the dependence of sensing behavior on the time. The PL intensity of BQDs was examined with the passage of time at a fixed wavelength i.e., 340 nm in the presence of hypochlorite ion and it was observed to be diminishing with time. The PL intensity quenched exponentially with time with R2 = 0.94251. Even though the PL intensity decreased with time but after 200 s it remained stable, so the response time can be stated as 200 s. Hence, the rests of the experiments were performed after 200 s.

To claim the specificity of the probe, competitive studies were performed where the response of BQDs to detect hypochlorite ion was checked in the simultaneous presence of other ionic species. (Fig. 5 (a)). No significant interference could be seen with any other competitive ions indicating it to be selective even in the co-existence of other species. The hypochlorite quenches the PL intensity of bare BQDs to almost 98%. A parallel quenching trend was analyzed even in the co-existence of competitive ions. The whole mechanism of the sensing is illustrated in the graphical abstract (Fig. 5 (b)).

A simple and facile hydrothermal route has been used to synthesize BQDs by using biocompatible BSA and Citric acid. The synthesized BQDs show an emission response that was independent of the excitation wavelength affirmed the absence of any defective state. Optical studies confirmed that the probe endured n→π* and π→π* transitions. FT-IR studies confirmed the presence of various functional groups. XRD studies revealed the amorphous nature of QDs. TEM studies showed the size of BQDs to be below 10 nm. These BQDs were exposed to different metal ions but shows selectivity towards hypochlorite ions. The LOD for hypochlorite ions is calculated and found out to be 35.39 μM.

Ekta Sharma: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Devika Vashisht: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Vaishali Thakur: Writing – review & editing. Aseem Vashisht: Resources, Software. S.K. Mehta: Supervision, Methodology, Project administration. Kulvinder Singh: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank SAIF/CIL, Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing the necessary research facilities. DV would like to thank CSIR (open) SRF for Fellowship.
